question: Define a moving average process of order q, denoted as MA(q). Explain the properties of its mean, variance, and autocorrelation function (ACF). Why is an MA(q) process always stationary?
expectedPoints: Definition: X_t = μ + ε_t + θ₁ε_{t-1} + ... + θ_qε_{t-q}
ACF cuts off after lag q
Stationarity: Defined as a linear combination of a white noise process, which is stationary, so MA(q) is always stationary for any finite q and parameter values.

question: Compare and contrast the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) and Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF) for an MA(1) process and an AR(1) process. What are the key identifying features of each?
expectedPoints: MA(1) ACF: One significant spike at lag 1, then cuts off to zero.
MA(1) PACF: Decays exponentially or in a damped sinusoidal pattern.
AR(1) ACF: Decays exponentially towards zero.
AR(1) PACF: One significant spike at lag 1, then cuts off to zero.
The ACF/PACF patterns are inverses for pure MA and pure AR processes, which helps in model identification.

question: Describe the concept of invertibility in the context of an MA(1) process. Why is it an important property? Write the equation for an invertible MA(1) process and show how it can be inverted to an infinite autoregressive (AR(∞)) representation.
expectedPoints: Definition: Invertibility means the MA process can be equivalently represented as an infinite AR process.
Importance: It ensures a unique model and that observations depend more on recent shocks than distant ones.
Equation: X_t = μ + ε_t + θε_{t-1} where |θ| < 1 for invertibility.
Inversion: ε_t = (X_t - μ) - θ(X_{t-1} - μ) + θ²(X_{t-2} - μ) - ... (using the lag operator or repeated substitution).
